questions.question: What do the three numbers on fertiliser containers represent?
options: The price, weight, and brand of the fertiliser
The percentage by weight of macronutrients N, P, and K
The manufacturing date, batch number, and expiry
The water content, pH level, and salt concentration
questions.correctExplanation: The three numbers indicate the percentage by weight of the macronutrients Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K) available to plants.
questions.wrongExplanation: The three numbers represent the NPK (Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium) percentage by weight, not manufacturing details.

questions.question: In what form is Nitrogen quoted on fertiliser labels?
options: Ammonia (NH₃) or Nitrate (NO₃) or a mixture of both
questions.hint: The text mentions two specific forms
questions.correctExplanation: Nitrogen is quoted as either Ammonia (NH₃) or Nitrate (NO₃) or a mixture of both - forms that are soluble and available to plants.
questions.wrongExplanation: Nitrogen is quoted as Ammonia (NH₃) or Nitrate (NO₃), not as pure gas or just urea.

questions.question: Phosphorus in fertiliser analysis is expressed in what form?
questions.correctExplanation: Phosphorus is expressed as Phosphorus Pentoxide (P₂O₅), which is the standard form for fertiliser analysis.
questions.wrongExplanation: Phosphorus is expressed as Phosphorus Pentoxide (P₂O₅), not elemental phosphorus or phosphoric acid.

questions.question: Potassium in fertiliser analysis is expressed in what form?
questions.hint: Similar to phosphorus, it's an oxide
questions.correctExplanation: Potassium is expressed as Potassium Oxide (K₂O) in fertiliser analysis.
questions.wrongExplanation: Potassium is expressed as Potassium Oxide (K₂O), matching the format used for phosphorus.

questions.question: How much Nitrogen is in a 25kg bag of 20:40:10 fertiliser?
questions.correctExplanation: A 20:40:10 analysis means 20% N, 40% P, 10% K. 20% of 25kg = 5kg of Nitrogen.
questions.wrongExplanation: With 20% nitrogen in a 25kg bag: 25kg × 0.20 = 5kg of Nitrogen.

questions.question: What does a fertiliser with analysis 3:3:6 and 5:5:10 have in common?
options: They have the same nutrient ratio of 1:1:2
They contain the same total weight of nutrients
They are both controlled release fertilisers
questions.hint: Divide each number by its lowest common factor
questions.correctExplanation: Both fertilisers have a nutrient ratio of 1:1:2. 3:3:6 divides by 3 → 1:1:2, and 5:5:10 divides by 5 → 1:1:2.
questions.wrongExplanation: Both fertilisers reduce to a 1:1:2 nutrient ratio when broken down to lowest common factors.

questions.question: What is another name for 'Nutrient Ratio'?
questions.correctExplanation: Nutrient ratio is also known as PER (Plant Food Ratio), often given as recommendations for fertiliser applications.
questions.wrongExplanation: Nutrient ratio is also called PER (Plant Food Ratio), as mentioned in the text.

questions.question: Which statement about inorganic fertilisers is TRUE?
options: They are always organic in origin
They are slow release by nature
They produce a rapid response in plant growth
questions.correctExplanation: Inorganic fertilisers contain nutrients in a form readily available for plant use, producing a rapid response in plant growth.
questions.wrongExplanation: Inorganic fertilisers are mined/quarried/manufactured, act quickly, have consistent analysis, and uniform particle size.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an exception to slow-release organic fertilisers?
options: Both A and B are exceptions
questions.hint: The text mentions two fast-acting exceptions
questions.correctExplanation: Dried blood and urea are the exceptions that are fast-acting. Bone meal is a typical slow-release organic fertiliser.
questions.wrongExplanation: Dried blood and urea are specifically mentioned as fast-acting exceptions to slow-release organic fertilisers.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of organic fertilisers regarding plant safety?
options: There is less risk of scorch to plant leaves or roots
They have more consistent nutrient content
questions.correctExplanation: Organic fertilisers release nutrients slowly, so there is less risk of scorch (fertiliser burn) to plant leaves or roots.
questions.wrongExplanation: Organic fertilisers have less risk of scorch because nutrients must be converted by soil organisms before becoming available.

questions.question: What characterises slow-release fertiliser particle size?
options: All particles are exactly the same size
Larger particles take longer to break down, extending availability
Particles dissolve immediately on contact with water
Particle size has no effect on release rate
questions.correctExplanation: Slow-release organic fertilisers vary in particle size. Larger particles take longer to break down, extending the period of nutrient availability.
questions.wrongExplanation: Larger particles of slow-release fertiliser take longer to break down, which extends the period of nutrient availability.

questions.question: How does controlled-release fertiliser work?
options: It releases all nutrients at once when applied
It has a climate-controlled mechanism that adjusts release based on temperature and moisture
It only releases nutrients when manually activated
It requires soil organisms to break it down
questions.correctExplanation: Controlled-release fertiliser has a mechanism that releases nutrients based on climate - as temperature rises and moisture is available, more nutrients are released.
questions.wrongExplanation: Controlled-release fertiliser is structured to release nutrients over months based on temperature and moisture conditions.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of traditional/conventional release fertilisers?
options: They release nutrients over several years
They have no controlling factors - nutrients release, peak and finish quickly
They only release nutrients at night
questions.correctExplanation: Traditional/conventional release fertilisers have no controlling factors. The nutrients release, peak and finish in a short time, often in weeks.
questions.wrongExplanation: Traditional release fertilisers have no controlling factors and release nutrients quickly, often finishing in a matter of weeks.

questions.question: What particle size range characterises 'prilled' fertiliser?
questions.correctExplanation: Prilled fertiliser has a particle size of 1.5 - 2.0mm, made by cooling liquid into solid pellets, resulting in round, free-flowing fertiliser.
questions.wrongExplanation: Prilled fertiliser particles are 1.5-2.0mm, smaller than granular (2-4mm).

questions.question: What particle size range characterises 'granular' fertiliser?
questions.correctExplanation: Granular fertiliser has a particle size of 2 - 4mm and is free from dust.
questions.wrongExplanation: Granular fertiliser particles are 2-4mm, larger than prilled (1.5-2.0mm).

questions.question: Why might liquid fertiliser be prone to leaching?
options: As it is soluble, it can quickly wash away with water
It evaporates before plants can use it
It reacts with air to form gas
questions.correctExplanation: Liquid fertilisers are soluble and have a quick response, but this solubility means they can be prone to leaching (washing away) with water.
questions.wrongExplanation: Liquid fertilisers are soluble, which allows quick uptake but also makes them prone to leaching away with water.

questions.question: What type of fertiliser is recommended for Spring/Summer turf?
options: Low in N, high in K
questions.correctExplanation: Spring/summer turf fertiliser is high in N (Nitrogen) to compensate for N lost due to removal of clippings and to aid recovery from wear.
questions.wrongExplanation: Spring/summer fertiliser is high in Nitrogen to replace nutrients lost from clipping removal and support growth.

questions.question: What type of fertiliser is recommended for Autumn/Winter turf?
options: Low in N, high in K
questions.hint: Think about hardening grass for cold
questions.correctExplanation: Autumn/winter fertiliser is low in N, high in K (Potassium). This hardens up the grass to withstand frost and wear.
questions.wrongExplanation: Autumn/winter fertiliser is low in N, high in K to harden grass against frost and wear.

questions.question: What is 'base dressing'?
options: Fertiliser applied to soil BEFORE sowing grass seed or laying turf
questions.correctExplanation: Base dressing is fertiliser applied to the soil before sowing grass seed or laying turf - establishing the nutrient foundation.
questions.wrongExplanation: Base dressing is applied to soil BEFORE sowing grass seed or laying turf, as opposed to top dressing.

questions.question: What is 'top dressing'?
options: Fertiliser applied to the soil surface
Fertiliser mixed into the soil before planting
questions.correctExplanation: Top dressing is fertiliser applied to established turf (existing grass), as opposed to base dressing which is applied before planting.
questions.wrongExplanation: Top dressing is applied to established turf, while base dressing is applied before sowing or laying turf.
